Audio:
Speaker 1: Um, I'm gonna go get you a glass of milk, and then we'll get started on your reading, okay?

Speaker 2: Okay, sweetie, why don't you grab a banana for now? You can eat it while Daddy reads with your brother.

Speaker 3: Actually, I had a banana, Mommy, but I want applesauce instead.

Speaker 4: Mommy, look, I drew a picture of a snowman on the window.

Speaker 5: Me too, me too!

Speaker 2: That's wonderful, sweetie, but let's focus on Daddy's reading first, okay?

Speaker 1: Yeah, so, uh, where were we? Ah yeah, reading. So, it's, uh, "The Snowy Day" by Ezra Jack Keats.

Speaker 3: But why did the boy have to walk all the way to school? Can't he just, like, take a taxi?

Speaker 1: Well, it's, uh, a different time period, and back then, people didn't have cars like we do now.

Speaker 4: I want to go outside and play in the snow!

Speaker 2: Not now, sweetie, it's still snowing heavily, and you know how slippery it gets.

Speaker 5: No, I wanna go out!

Speaker 1: Hey, hey, let's focus on reading, okay? So, what's happening in the story?

Speaker 3: I know that, Daddy, he's going to go sledding down a big hill!

Speaker 4: Mommy, look, I saw a bird outside the window!

Speaker 2: Oh, sweetie, I think it was a sparrow. Aren't they just the cutest?

Speaker 5: Look, bird!

Speaker 1: Okay, so let's keep reading. Where was I? Ah yeah, "He walked all the way through the snow...".

Speaker 3: But why did he have to walk so far? That's not fair!

Speaker 2: Actually, honey, it's not about being fair, it's about understanding the story.

Speaker 4: I want to tell a story, Mommy!

Speaker 2: Okay, sweetie, tell Mommy a story.

Speaker 5: Me too, me too!

Speaker 1: I think we're getting a little off track, guys. So, let's get back to reading.

Speaker 3: Yeah, yeah, I know, but can we go sledding after, please, Daddy?

Speaker 1: We'll see, buddy. Let's finish the story first.

Speaker 4: I want applesauce now!

Speaker 2: Okay, sweetie, I'll get you some applesauce. How about after we finish reading?

Speaker 5: No, I want it now!
Image: A room with light brown wooden flooring in a herringbone pattern, partially covered by a cream-colored area rug. A beige sectional sofa with a brown leather armchair sits near a white oval coffee table with a closed book and a water bottle on it. A wooden bookshelf behind the sofa holds several books and a framed family picture.

Audio:
Speaker 1: So, I'm gonna grab a coffee, it's, uh, already 11:23, I need to get back to the office, I've got this project due next week and, you know, it's just not going to get done if I don't focus.

Speaker 2: (interrupting) Dad, look at this!

Speaker 1: (to Speaker 2) What is it, buddy? You know we're busy, can't you see Mom and I talking?

Speaker 3: (young child) I wanna show Daddy, I found a cookie on the counter!

Speaker 1: (to Speaker 3) Not now, sweetie, Mom can see you, she's busy too, let's talk about this later, okay?

Speaker 2: (to Speaker 1) So, how was the meeting with John this morning?

Speaker 1: Um, it was good, we're making progress on the Smith account, but I'm still worried about the deadline, it's tight, you know?

Speaker 3: (young child) Can I have a cookie, please?

Speaker 2: (to Speaker 3) Not now, we'll have a snack later, let's focus on Daddy's work.

Speaker 1: (to Speaker 2) Yeah, so basically, we're all set for the meeting tomorrow, just gotta finalize the presentation.

Speaker 4: (young child) Mommy, I need help with my homework!

Speaker 2: (to Speaker 4) Oh, okay, sweetie, Mom will help you with that, I'll stay here and catch up with Dad.

Speaker 1: So, you know, the weather's not great today, it's pretty chilly and overcast, glad I grabbed my jacket.

Speaker 3: (young child) I wanna go outside and play!

Speaker 2: (to Speaker 3) Not today, buddy, it's too cold, you can play with your toys instead.

Speaker 1: Yeah, you know, I was thinking about the kids' school next week, have we finalized the schedule for their activities?

Speaker 2: (to Speaker 1) Um, I think so, let me check my calendar.

Speaker 4: (young child) I don't like my homework, it's boring!

Speaker 2: (to Speaker 4) Okay, sweetie, let's talk about it, what's not working for you?

Speaker 1: (to Speaker 4) You know, it's just that school's been keeping them pretty busy lately, I'm glad we have a few days off next week.

Speaker 2: (to Speaker 1) Yeah, me too, we can finally catch up on some things around the house.

Speaker 3: (young child) Can I have a cookie now?

Speaker 2: (to Speaker 3) Not yet, let's finish talking to Daddy first.

Speaker 1: So, I'm gonna grab this coffee and get back to work, see you guys later.

Speaker 2: (to Speaker 1) Okay, love you, see you soon.

Speaker 4: (young child) I love you too, Daddy!
Image: A view of a cafeteria with a beige linoleum flooring and a few wooden tables. A stainless steel coffee machine stands against a wall, with a steaming coffee cup in a hand. A brown leather armchair with a blue cushion and a side table with a glass vase and a few office magazines sit near a large window with a frosted glass pane.

Audio:
Speaker 2: Okay so, like, I was thinking about the Smith project, and we need to have that meeting on Monday, right? And then I was thinking, how about we make some tea first, it's so cold outside.

Speaker 1 (young child): Look at this! Mommy, Mommy!

Speaker 2: Okay sweetie, what's that? *pause* Oh, it's your dinosaur toy. Um, yeah, go play with it a bit, I'll make some tea.

Speaker 1 (young child): I wanna help!

Speaker 2: No, no, sweetie, you sit down. Remember when we had those apples from the farmer's market last weekend? Okay, so I was thinking about making a salad for lunch tomorrow.

Speaker 1 (young child): Mommy, I'm hungry!

Speaker 2: Okay, I'll make you a sandwich later. So, uh, back to the meeting, we need to finalize the report by then.

Speaker 3 (older child): Can we have pizza for dinner tonight?

Speaker 2: Um, let's see. Okay so, remember when we said we'd make that pizza on the weekend? Yeah, how about we stick to the plan and make it then?

Speaker 1 (young child): I wanna pizza!

Speaker 2: Okay, sweetie, one more minute. So, uh, the Smith project, we need to make a presentation on Monday at 2 pm.

Speaker 3 (older child): Can we watch that new movie on Netflix tonight?

Speaker 2: Um, we'll see. Let's finish this tea first. It's so cold and dry outside.

Speaker 1 (young child): Mommy, I drew a picture!

Speaker 2: Oh, great! Let me see. It's a... um... what is that supposed to be?

Speaker 3 (older child): It's a cat, Mommy!

Speaker 2: Yeah, it's a great cat! Okay, so I was thinking, how about we make some plans for the kids' school projects this weekend?

Speaker 1 (young child): I wanna make a picture!

Speaker 2: Okay, sweetie, go get some paper and crayons, and I'll help you. So, uh, the school projects, we need to decide on a theme and...

Speaker 3 (older child): Can we have ice cream for dessert tonight?

Speaker 2: Um, let's see. We can make some hot chocolate later instead, okay?
Image: A room with light brown wooden flooring in a herringbone pattern. A cream-colored armchair with a blue blanket draped over it sits near a white oval coffee table with a book and a water bottle. On a stainless steel counter, a kettle steams as it boils water for a cup of tea in a ceramic mug with a floral pattern, next to a packet of herbal tea on a wooden table top.

Audio:
Speaker 1: Hey, um, I'm thinking about what to make for dinner. You know, since we're all tired from work and school today.

Speaker 2: (background noise, kids arguing) I'm bored, Mommy!

Speaker 1: Anyway, I was thinking maybe something simple, like beef noodles or stir-fry. But, you know, the thing is, I'm not really in the mood to cook.

Speaker 3: (entering the conversation) Dad, can we have pizza? Pleeease?

Speaker 1: Well, sweetie, we can talk about it, but you know, your mom and I were just discussing dinner.

Speaker 2: (interrupting) Actually, dear, I was thinking we could make some soup instead.

Speaker 1: Oh, soup sounds good too, but, you know, I'm a bit tired today and it's cold outside. Overcast and chilly.

Speaker 3: (excitedly) Look at this! I found a new Lego piece!

Speaker 2: (laughing) Ah, congratulations! Great find!

Speaker 1: (trying to refocus) Anyway, about dinner, maybe we can, uh, compromise? Make something easy and simple.

Speaker 4: (in the background) Mom, can I have a cookie?

Speaker 2: Not now, dear, Daddy's talking.

Speaker 1: (sighs) I know, work's been stressful lately, but we need to find a better balance, you know? So we can enjoy our time together.

Speaker 3: (interrupting) Can we watch a movie after dinner, Dad?

Speaker 1: That sounds like a great idea, sweetie. But, you know, we need to make sure your mom and I finish our work first.

Speaker 2: (concurring) Yes, and we need to discuss your school projects too.

Speaker 4: (excitedly) I'm going to make a snowman tomorrow!

Speaker 2: (encouragingly) That sounds like fun! Make sure to wear your warmest coat, okay?

Speaker 1: (getting distracted) Snowman, yeah, we should, uh, take some pictures. You know, for the, uh, family album.

Speaker 3: (curious) Can I hold your phone, Dad?

Speaker 2: (handing the phone over) Here, sweetie, take the phone and, uh, make sure your dad takes good photos.

Speaker 1: (sighs) I'm glad we're all home safe and sound, you know. Now let's just relax and enjoy our evening together.

Speaker 4: (excitedly) Yeah! And I have a new puzzle to do!

Speaker 2: (laughing) Alright, one puzzle at a time, dear.

Speaker 1: (concluding) Yeah, let's just take it easy tonight, okay?

Speaker 2: Sounds like a plan to me.
Image: A view of a narrow alleyway near Jing'an Temple features gray stone flooring. A worn wooden bench with a backrest in a traditional Chinese style is situated against a wall, covered with a faded red awning. A metal streetlamp casts a faint glow on the wet pavement.

Audio:
Speaker 1: You know, I'm thinking about making pasta or chicken fettuccine for dinner, what do you think, honey?

Speaker 2: Actually, that's a great question. What's the schedule looking like for tonight? Do we have any sports practice or activities?

Speaker 1: Yeah, the kids have their piano practice at 6, but that's, uh, an hour from now, so we've got some time. I can make a quick sauce for the pasta.

Speaker 2: Okay, so pasta sounds good, but can we make sure to get some veggies in there too, maybe some stir-fry with the chicken?

Speaker 3: But why do we have to have vegetables, mommy? I don't like them.

Speaker 2: Sweetie, you know why we have to eat our veggies. They're good for us. How about we make some broccoli on the side?

Speaker 3: Actually, I know that broccoli is good for us. I learned that in school.

Speaker 1: That's right, buddy. You're a smart kid. So, I'm thinking, pasta or chicken?

Speaker 4: Mommy, look! I made a picture on the couch cushion with my crayons. Can I show it to you?

Speaker 2: Okay, sweetie, show me later, okay? Your dad's trying to decide on dinner.

Speaker 1: Yeah, yeah, later little one. So, pasta or chicken?

Speaker 4: I want chicken, please please, can we have chicken?

Speaker 2: Okay, let's compromise. We'll make pasta with a simple sauce and a side of chicken. Everyone happy?

Speaker 5: Me too, me too, I want chicken!

Speaker 1: Okay, buddy, you can have a little bit of chicken. Your mom will cut it up for you.

Speaker 4: That's not fair, why I don't get all the chicken?

Speaker 2: Sweetie, everyone gets a little bit of chicken. It's not a big deal.

Speaker 5: Look, look! I found a penny on the floor!

Speaker 3: Actually, I found a penny too, yesterday, it was in the living room.

Speaker 1: That's great, kids. Keep looking for those lost treasures. Okay, everyone, let's get started on dinner.

Speaker 2: Okay, sounds good. I'll start chopping the vegetables and your dad can make the pasta.

Speaker 1: Sounds like a plan. I'll get the water boiling and we can get this dinner going.

Speaker 4: Mommy, can I help with dinner? I want to be the chef!

Speaker 2: Of course, sweetie, you can help. But remember to wash your hands first.

Speaker 3: Can I be the chef too, please? I know how to make a great sauce.

Speaker 1: Okay, buddy, you can be the sauce artist. Your mom will supervise you.

Speaker 5: No, no, I want to be the chef!

Speaker 2: Okay, okay, everyone can help. We'll make it a team effort.

Speaker 1: That's the spirit. Teamwork makes the dream work.

Speaker 4: I don't want to share the chef duties, mommy!

Speaker 2: Sweetie, it's okay to share. We all have different jobs to do.

Speaker 5: Look, look, a bird outside!

Speaker 3: That's a great bird, I love watching birds. Can we get some birdseed?

Speaker 1: Yeah, we'll get some birdseed on the way home from piano practice tomorrow.

Speaker 2: Sounds like a plan. Okay, let's get back to dinner. Who's helping with the veggies?

Speaker 4: I am, I am! I want to be the veggie helper!

Speaker 3: Can I help too, please?

Speaker 1: Okay, buddy, you can help with the veggie chopper.

Speaker 2: Great, let's get started on this dinner.
